A December 2022 survey estimated 22 birds on Granite Island,<ref name="washington2022" /> with a different study putting the number at 26 that year. After 28 birds were counted in mid-2023, researchers were hopeful of a recovery in numbers.<ref name="horn2023" /> A count by volunteers in late 2025 revealed the number of birds to be around 36, up from 30 the previous year. There were 18 active burrows.<ref name=horn2025/>

==== Tasmania ====
Tasmania has Australia's largest Australian little penguin population, with estimates ranging from 110,000 to 190,000 breeding pairs, of which less than 5% are found on mainland Tasmania.<ref>[https://dpipwe.tas.gov.au/wildlife-management/marine-conservation-program/little-penguins-in-tasmania/about-little-penguins About Little Penguins]  Department of Primary Industries, Parks, Water and Environment (Tasmania),  19 March 2020. Retrieved 31 December 2020.</ref>  Roughly 20,000 pairs occur on [[Babel Island]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Birds of world significance: Babel Island Group, Tasmania |url=http://www.birdata.com.au/printibaWBDBBirdList.do?sitRecID=23907 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120426043141/http://www.birdata.com.au/printibaWBDBBirdList.do?sitRecID=23907 |archive-date=26 April 2012 |access-date=29 November 2011 |work=Atlas of Australian Birds |publisher=Birds Australia}}</ref> Conservation activities, education campaigns, and measures to prevent dog attacks on Australian little penguin rookeries have been implemented.<ref>[https://dpipwe.tas.gov.au/wildlife-management/marine-conservation-program/little-penguins-in-tasmania/monitoring-and-protection Little Penguin Monitoring and Protection Measures]  Department of Primary Industries, Parks, Water and Environment (Tasmania), 14 February 2020. Retrieved 31 December 2020.</ref>

==== Victoria ====
[[File:20091121_Little_Penguin_on_rock_at_St_Kilda_Breakwater_(left_side_view).jpg|thumb|Little penguin at night at the [[St Kilda, Victoria|St Kilda]] breakwater]]
The largest colony of little penguins in Victoria is located at [[Phillip Island]], where the nightly 'parade' of penguins across Summerland Beach has been a major tourist destination, and more recently a major conservation effort, since the 1920s. Phillip Island is home to an estimated 32,000 breeding adults.<ref>{{cite web |title=About Little Penguins |url=http://penguinfoundation.org.au/about-little-penguins/#General1 |access-date=20 June 2019 |website=penguinfoundation.org.au}}</ref> Australian little penguins can also be seen in the vicinity of the [[St Kilda, Victoria|St Kilda]] pier and breakwater in the inner suburbs of [[Melbourne]]. The breakwater is home to a colony of little penguins which have been the subject of a conservation study since 1986.<ref>{{cite web |title=Conservation and Research Strategy 2007 |url=http://stkildapenguins.com.au/skp/wp-content/uploads/2012/07/Conservation-and-Research-Strategy-20071.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140126181911/http://stkildapenguins.com.au/skp/wp-content/uploads/2012/07/Conservation-and-Research-Strategy-20071.pdf |archive-date=26 January 2014 |access-date=2 December 2014 |website=stkildapenguins.com.au |publisher=Earthcare St Kilda}}</ref> As of 2020 the colony is 1,400 breeding adults and growing.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Topsfield |first=Jewel |date=2020-02-26 |title=Rangers roped in to help wrangle crowds at St Kilda's penguin colony |url=https://www.theage.com.au/politics/victoria/rangers-roped-in-to-help-wrangle-crowds-at-st-kilda-s-penguin-colony-20200221-p5436n.html |access-date=2020-07-12 |website=The Age |language=en}}</ref>

Little penguin habitats also exist at a number of other locations, including [[London Bridge (Victoria)|London Bridge]] and [[The Twelve Apostles (Victoria)|The Twelve Apostles]] along the [[Great Ocean Road]], [[Wilsons Promontory]], and [[Gabo Island]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Eudyptula minor – Little Penguin |url=http://www.environment.gov.au/cgi-bin/sprat/public/publicspecies.pl?taxon_id=1085 |access-date=3 December 2014 |website=environment.gov.au |publisher=Australian government - Department of Environment}}</ref>

==== Western Australia ====
The largest colony of Australian little penguins in Western Australia is believed to be located on [[Penguin Island (Western Australia)|Penguin Island]], where an estimated 1,000 pairs nest during winter.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Visitor Information: About Penguin Island |url=http://www.penguinisland.com.au/visitor-information.html# |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140718021443/http://penguinisland.com.au/visitor-information.html |archive-date=18 July 2014 |access-date=27 July 2014 |website=penguinisland.com.au}}</ref> Penguins are also known to nest on [[Garden Island (Western Australia)|Garden Island]] and [[Carnac Island]] which lie north of Penguin Island. Many islands along Western Australia's southern coast are likely to support little penguin colonies, though the status of these populations is largely unknown. An account of little penguins on Bellinger Island published in 1928 numbered them in their thousands. Visiting naturalists in November 1986 estimated the colony at 20 breeding pairs.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Smith |first1=L. E. |last2=Johnstone |first2=R. E. |year=1987 |title=Corella - Seabird Islands No. 179 |url=http://www.absa.asn.au/Corella/CVol11/C11395.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141209182340/http://www.absa.asn.au/Corella/CVol11/C11395.pdf |archive-date=9 December 2014 |access-date=8 December 2014 |website=Australian Bird Study Association inc.}}</ref> The account named another substantial colony 12 miles from Bellinger Island and the same distance from Cape Pasley.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Douglas |first=Alfred |date=1 April 1928 |title=The little blue penguin |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article58350254 |access-date=19 October 2015 |work=Sunday Times |location=Perth, Western Australia}}</ref> Little penguins are known to breed on some islands of the [[Recherche Archipelago]], including [[Woody Island (Western Australia)|Woody Island]] where day-tripping tourists can view the animals. A penguin colony exists on [[Mistaken Island]] in [[King George Sound (Western Australia)|King George Sound]] near [[Albany, Western Australia|Albany]].<ref>{{Cite news |date=1 January 1938 |title=Albany Suspects Incendiarism ALBANY, Saturday. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article83419440 |access-date=7 November 2015 |newspaper=The Daily News |page=7}}</ref> Historical accounts of little penguins on Newdegate Island at the mouth of [[Deep River (Western Australia)|Deep River]]<ref>{{Cite news |date=11 November 1898 |title=CHAPTER XII.—1841. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article67047401 |access-date=7 November 2015 |newspaper=The Inquirer and Commercial News |page=4}}</ref> and on [[Breaksea Island (Western Australia)|Breaksea Island]] in [[King George Sound (Western Australia)|King George Sound]] also exist. West Australian little penguins have been found to forage as far as 150 miles north of Geraldton (south of Denham and Shark Bay).<ref name=":10" />

=== New Zealand ===
[[File:Little_Penguin_swimming_in_the_water.jpg|thumb|Swimming in [[Otago Harbour]]]]
While the main range of this penguin is in Australia, at least one population of ''Eudyptula novaehollandiae'' exists in [[Otago]], which is located on the east coast of New Zealand's [[South Island]].<ref name="MessageToEagle2">{{cite web |date=16 December 2015 |title=Australian and New Zealand Little Blue Penguins are Different Species |url=http://www.messagetoeagle.com/australian-and-new-zealand-little-blue-penguins-are-different-species/ |access-date=12 May 2016 |website=MessagetoEagle.com}}</ref> The chicks are raised in nests constructed in burrows along the shoreline, both dug by ''Eudyptula minor'' and by other animals. ''Eudyptula minor'' does not occur in [[Otago]], which is located on the east coast of New Zealand's [[South Island]]. The Australian species ''[[Eudyptula novaehollandiae]]'' occurs in Otago.<ref name="MessageToEagle2"/>

As ''E. novaehollandiae'' was originally endemic to Australia, the arrival of the Australian species in New Zealand was determined to have occurred roughly between AD 1500 and 1900. When the ''E. minor'' population declined in New Zealand, it left a genetic opening for ''E. novaehollandiae''. The decrease of ''E. minor'' was most likely due to anthropogenic effects, such as being hunted by humans as well as introduced predators,<ref>{{cite web |last1=Grosser |first1=Stefanie |title=NZ's southern little penguins are recent Aussie invaders: Otago research |url=http://www.otago.ac.nz/news/news/otago515801.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230420052822/https://www.otago.ac.nz/news/news/otago515801.html |archive-date=20 April 2023 |access-date=11 May 2016 |website=University of Otago |publisher=University of Otago: Department of Zoology}}</ref> including dogs brought from overseas. It has been determined that the population of ''Eudyptula novaehollandiae'' in Otago arrived even more recently than previously estimated due to [[Multispecies coalescent process|multilocus coalescent analyses]].<ref name="coalescent3"/>

Outside of the Otago region, all colonies are expected to belong to the subspecies ''Eudyptula minor.'' Many of these colonies are smaller and more patchily distributed than larger ''Eudyptula novaehollandiae'' colonies that exist in Australia and Otago. Extensive research exists on Philip Island<ref>Giling Darren, Reina Richard D., Hogg Zoe (2008) Anthropogenic influence on an urban colony of the little penguin Eudyptula minor. Marine and Freshwater Research 59, 647-651.</ref> and Oamaru colonies as they are sites of large colonies which attract large groups of tourists<ref name="Mattern">Mattern, Thomas & Wilson, Kerry-Jayne. (2018). New Zealand penguins - current knowledge and research priorities. 10.13140/RG.2.2.16180.50564/3.</ref>
Population size and trends of colonies in New Zealand remain poorly documented. What are known as colonies in New Zealand commonly consist of smaller fragmented groups in comparison to Australia's larger colonies, some with <10 breeding pairs. This is largely attributed to NZs fragmented coastline separating the larger colonies. This is commonly seen in [[Kaikōura]] where 6–7 smaller colonies have been found along 1.7% of coastline<ref name="Mattern" /><ref>Cargill, C. P., Judkins, A. G., & Weir, J. S. (2020). Distribution of little penguins (Eudyptula minor) along the greater Kaikōura coastline, New Zealand. New Zealand Journal of Marine and Freshwater Research, 56(1), 43–58.</ref>

=== Outside of Australasia ===
Sightings of ''Eudyptula'' have been reported from Chile, where they are known as ''pingüino enano'', ''pingüino pequeño'', or ''pingüino hada.'' These include on [[Chañaral Island|Isla Chañaral]] in 1995; at the Playa de Santo Domingo, [[San Antonio, Chile|San Antonio]], 16 March 1997; 2005 in Chilean Antarctica; 2009 [[Magdalena Island, Magallanes Region|Isla Magdalena]] in the [[Magellan Straits]]; and in 2015 at Caleta Abarca beach in [[Vina del Mar]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Pinguino enano |url=https://www.avesdechile.cl/265.htm |access-date=30 October 2023 |website=Aves de Chile |language=es}}</ref><ref name="Ramel 2023 p852">{{cite web |last=Ramel |first=Gordon |date=13 July 2023 |title=Little Penguins or Fairy Penguins (Eudyptula Minor) |url=https://earthlife.net/little-or-fairy-penguins-eudyptula-minor/ |access-date=30 October 2023 |website=Earth Life}}</ref>

In April 2005 the first recorded sighting of a living little penguin occurred on [[Ichaboe Island]] in Namibia.<ref name="Ramel 2023 p852" />

== Behaviour ==
=== Feeding ===
[[File:Eudyptula minor, Auckland, New Zealand imported from iNaturalist photo 430247598.jpg|thumb|Feeding on [[Hyporhamphus ihi|New Zealand piper]] (''Hyporhamphus ihi''), [[Auckland]]]]
Little penguins feed by hunting small [[Clupeidae|clupeoid]] fish, [[cephalopod]]s, and [[crustacean]]s, for which they travel and dive quite extensively<ref name="Flemming"/><ref>[http://www.aucklandcouncil.govt.nz/EN/environmentwaste/naturalenvironment/Documents/biodiversitylittlepenguinfactsheet.pdf "Little Penguin Factsheet"] ''Auckland Council'', New Zealand (28 February 2014). Retrieved 2014-07-26.</ref> including to the sea floor.  Researcher Tom Montague studied a Victorian population for two years in order to understand its feeding patterns. Montague's analysis revealed a penguin diet consisting of 76% fish and 24% squid. Nineteen fish species were recorded, with [[Sardinops|pilchard]] and [[anchovy]] dominating. The fish were usually less than 10 cm long and often post-larval or juvenile. Less common little penguin prey include: [[crab]] larvae, [[eel]]s, jellyfish, and [[seahorse]]s.<ref name=":9511"/> In New Zealand, important little penguin prey items include [[Nototodarus sloanii|arrow squid]], [[slender sprat]], [[Graham's gudgeon]], [[red cod]], and [[ahuru]].<ref name="Flemming3">Flemming, S.A. (2013) "[http://nzbirdsonline.org.nz/species/little-penguin]". ''In Miskelly, C.M. (ed.)'' ''New Zealand Birds Online''</ref>

Little penguins are generally inshore feeders.<ref name="Numata">Numata, M; Davis, L & Renner, M (2000) "[Prolonged foraging trips and egg desertion in little penguins (''Eudyptula minor'')]". ''New Zealand Journal of Zoology'' '''27''': 291-298</ref> The use of data loggers has shown that in the diving behaviour of little penguins, 50% of dives go no deeper than 2 m, and the mean diving time is 21 seconds.<ref>Bethge, P; Nicol, S; Culik, BM & RP Wilson (1997) "[https://scholar.google.co.nz/scholar?hl=en&lr=&safe=off&q=little+penguin&btnG=Search Diving behaviour and energetics in breeding little penguins (''Eudyptula minor'')]". ''Journal of Zoology'' '''242''': 483-502</ref> In the 1980s, average little penguin dive time was estimated to be 23–24 seconds.<ref name=":9511"/> The maximum recorded depth and time submerged are 66.7 metres and 90 seconds respectively.<ref>Ropert-Coudert Y, Chiaradia A, Kato A (2006) "[http://docyaounde.free.fr/stock/pdf/yan06marorni.pdf An exceptionally deep dive by a Little Penguin Eudyptula minor]". ''Marine Ornithology'' '''34''': 71-74</ref> Tracking technology is allowing researchers from [[Institute for Marine and Antarctic Studies|IMAS]] and the [[University of Tasmania]] to garner new insights into the foraging behavior of little penguins.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Tasmanian Little Penguin study casts light on foraging behaviour - Institute for Marine and Antarctic Studies |url=https://www.imas.utas.edu.au/news/news-items/tasmanian-little-penguin-study-casts-light-on-foraging-behaviour |access-date=2020-07-13 |website=Institute for Marine and Antarctic Studies - University of Tasmania, Australia |language=en-AU |archive-date=10 June 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210610083437/https://www.imas.utas.edu.au/news/news-items/tasmanian-little-penguin-study-casts-light-on-foraging-behaviour |url-status=dead }}</ref>

Little penguins are central place foragers, meaning they will travel distances to forage but always return to the same nest or colony.<ref>Olsson, O., Helf, K. L., & Brown, J. S. (2008). A guide to central place effects in foraging [Review of A guide to central place effects in foraging]. Theoretical Population Biology, 74(1), 22–33</ref> They are also a species where both parents are required to raise chicks, and alternate foraging trips while the other is guarding and incubating the nest during the post guard stage. These stints can last anywhere between 1–10 days during incubation.<ref name="Mattern" /> Despite nesting on the shore, little penguins forage at sea and feed on a diet ranging from small schooling fish, to cephalopods, krill, and microzooplankton.<ref>Braidwood, J., Kunz, J., & Wilson, K. J. (2011). Effect of habitat features on the breeding success of the blue penguin (Eudyptula minor) on the West Coast of New Zealand. New Zealand Journal of Zoology, 38(2), 131–141.</ref> As the species is widely distributed across a range of habitats in New Zealand and Australia, variation in diet and foraging choice has also arisen. Important little penguin prey items include [[Nototodarus sloanii|arrow squid]], [[slender sprat]], [[Graham's gudgeon]], [[red cod]], and [[ahuru]].<ref name="Flemming3"/>

Since the year 2000, the diet of the Australian little penguins of [[Port Phillip]] has consisted mainly of [[Australian anchovy|Australian anchovies]] (the primary food source),<ref name="Dann">Dann, P., & Chambers, L. (2013). Ecological effects of climate change on little penguins Eudyptula minor and the potential economic impact on tourism [Review of Ecological effects of climate change on little penguins Eudyptula minor and the potential economic impact on tourism]. Climate Research, 58(1), 67–79. https://www.jstor.org/stable/24896130</ref> [[barracouta]], [[anchovy]], and [[Gould's squid]]. [[Sardine|Pilchards]] previously featured more prominently in southern Australian little penguin diets prior to mass sardine mortality events of the 1990s. These mass mortality events affected sardine stocks over 5,000 kilometres of coastline.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Chiaradia |first1=André |last2=Forero |first2=Manuela G. |last3=Hobson |first3=Keith A. |last4=Cullen |first4=J. Mike |title=Changes in diet and trophic position of a top predator 10 years after a mass mortality of a key prey |journal=ICES Journal of Marine Science |date=1 November 2010 |volume=67 |issue=8 |pages=1710–1720 |doi=10.1093/icesjms/fsq067|hdl=10261/59252 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Jellyfish including species in the genera ''[[Chrysaora]]'' and ''[[Cyanea (cnidarian)|Cyanea]]'' were found to be actively sought-out food items, while they previously had been thought to be only accidentally ingested. Similar preferences were found in the [[Adélie penguin]], [[yellow-eyed penguin]], and [[Magellanic penguin]].<ref>{{cite news |author=Christie Wilcox |date=15 September 2017 |title=Penguins Caught Feasting on an Unexpected Prey |url=http://news.nationalgeographic.com/2017/09/penguins-eat-jellyfish-unexpected-prey/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170916094933/http://news.nationalgeographic.com/2017/09/penguins-eat-jellyfish-unexpected-prey/ |archive-date=16 September 2017 |newspaper=National Geographic}}</ref> An important crustacean present in the little penguin diet is the krill, ''Nyctiphanes australis'', which surface-swarms during the day.<ref name=":9511"/>

Although the diet of the Philip Island colony has diversified to include selections of cephalopods and krill during the post guard stage of their life cycle (where greater amounts of energy is required for chick development and egg production), resident penguins predominantly rely on anchovies when more energy is required.<ref>Chiaradia, A., Forero, M. G., Hobson, K. A., Swearer, S., Hume, F., Dann, P., & Renwick, L. (2011). Diet segregation between two colonies of little penguins Eudyptula minor in southeast Australia [Review of Diet segregation between two colonies of little penguins Eudyptula minor in southeast Australia]. Austral Ecology, 37(5), 610–619.</ref>

The nature of their diet also impacts foraging methods, which may vary by colony depending on what food is available. When prey is larger and individuals are only catching 1-2 items at a time, they are more likely to hunt alone to reduce competition, whereas smaller and more mobile prey, or schooling prey species, promote group hunting to enable efficient encirclement.<ref>Sutton, G. J., Hoskins, A. J., & Arnould, J. P. Y. (2015). Benefits of Group Foraging Depend on Prey Type in a Small Marine Predator, the Little Penguin. PLoS ONE, 10(12).</ref> The Oamaru colony predominantly feeds on smaller schooling species such as sprat and gudgeon, while penguins from the Stewart/Codfish Island colonies more often hunt alone. The latter is likely linked to a predominantly cephalopods diet (58% of prey items at < 10 gm each).<ref name="Mattern" />

=== Foraging behaviour ===
[[file:Little Blue Penguins at the Arctic Research Centre Christchurch.jpg|thumb|Eudyptula minor foraging at the International Antarctic Centre in Christchurch]]

Like many penguin species, Australian little penguins spend the largest part of their day swimming and foraging at sea. The species may also forage at night but never visits burrows during the day, making it the only penguin whose presence on land is strictly [[Nocturnality|nocturnal]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Department of Conservation, Marine Ecosystems Team |title=Little penguin behaviour and ecosystem health |url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/globalassets/documents/conservation/marine-and-coastal/marine-protected-areas/monitoring-factsheets/little-penguin-monitoring.pdf |publisher=New Zealand Government |access-date=23 December 2024 |location=Wellington, New Zealand |date=July 2015}}</ref><ref name="Rodriguez2016">{{cite journal |last1=Rodríguez |first1=Airam |last2=Chiaradia |first2=André |last3=Wasiak |first3=Paula |last4=Renwick |first4=Leanne |last5=Dann |first5=Peter |title=Waddling on the Dark Side: Ambient Light Affects Attendance Behavior of Little Penguins |journal=Journal of Biological Rhythms |date=April 2016 |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=194–204 |doi=10.1177/0748730415626010 |pmid=26823445 |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0748730415626010 |access-date=23 December 2024|hdl=10261/132256 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> During the breeding and chick-rearing seasons, they travel within their home range to find food, but will return to their nest to feed both themselves and their chicks and return to their nests just after dusk. Thus, sunlight, moonlight, and artificial lights can affect the behaviour of attendance to the colony.<ref>Rodríguez, A., Chiaradia, A., Wasiak, P., Renwick, L., and Dann, P.(2016) "[http://jbr.sagepub.com/content/31/2/194 Waddling on the Dark Side: Ambient Light Affects Attendance Behavior of Little Penguins]." ''Journal of Biological Rhythms'' 31:194-204</ref> Their foraging range is limited by how long chicks can fast, and the high energetic of costs of constant travelling for individuals.<ref>Berlincourt, M., Arnould, J. P., & Y. (2015). Influence of environmental conditions on foraging behaviour and its consequences on reproductive performance in little penguins. Marine Biology, 162(7), 1485-1501.</ref>

This behaviour results in a small foraging range, and therefore a higher probability of competition when prey availability is more scarce.<ref name="Pelletier">Pelletier, L., Chiaradia, A., Kato, A., & Ropert-Coudert, Y. (2014). Fine-scale spatial age segregation in the limited foraging area of an inshore seabird species, the little penguin. Oecologia, 176(2), 399+.</ref> In order to survive, ''Eudyptula minor'' adapt to these constraints by increasing the plasticity and variability in their foraging behaviour, such as spatial, age, or diet based segregation, during breeding season when energy demands for both parents and chicks are at their highest.<ref>Diet segregation between two colonies of little penguins Eudyptulaminor in southeast Australia. Austral Ecology, 37: 610-619.</ref> During chick rearing, parents will make on average one day long foraging trips within a 30 km radius of their nest.<ref name="Pelletier" />

Research conducted on the Philip Island colony found the spatial segregation of foraging behaviour was primarily determined by age rather than biological sex.<ref name="Pelletier" /> Middle aged individuals foraged at greater distances from their nests and were able to dive greater distances, whereas older penguins were found to forage closer to the shore than middle aged adults.<ref>(Zimmer, I., Ropert-Coudert, Y., Kato, A., Ancel, A., & Chiaradia, A. (2011). Does Foraging Performance Change with Age in Female Little Penguins (Eudyptula minor)? PLoS One, 6(1))</ref><ref name="Pelletier" /> However, increased wind speeds negatively affect the little penguins' efficiency in foraging for chicks, for reasons not yet understood.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Saraux |first1=Claire |last2=Chiaradia |first2=Andre |last3=Salton |first3=Marcus |last4=Dann |first4=Peter |last5=Viblanc |first5=Vincent A |year=2016 |title=Negative effects of wind speed on individual foraging performance and breeding success in little penguins |url=http://archimer.ifremer.fr/doc/00318/42964/42469.pdf |journal=Ecological Monographs |volume=86 |issue=1 |pages=61–77 |bibcode=2016EcoM...86...61S |doi=10.1890/14-2124.1 |access-date=6 January 2017}}</ref> Australian little penguins preen their feathers to keep them waterproof. They do this by rubbing a tiny drop of oil onto every feather from a special gland above the tail.

When foraging in groups for small schooling prey, they were also observed to all be of a similar age cohort.<ref>Sutton, G. J., Hoskins, A. J., & Arnould, J. P. Y. (2015). Benefits of GroupForaging Depend on Prey Type in a Small Marine Predator, the Little Penguin. PLoS ONE, 10(12)</ref> If the groups are segregated by age, this is likely because they are at the same foraging ability and occupy the same approximate range.<ref name="Pelletier" />

=== Prey availability ===
Food availability appears to strongly influence the survival and breeding success of little penguin populations across their range.<ref name=":9511"/> Rising ocean temperatures have seen a trend towards earlier onset of breeding in'' Eudyptula minor'' which does not always align with the availability of their prey. This is because higher sea surface temperatures are associated with early onset of nesting, but also associated with lower nutrients and oxygen availability.<ref name="Dann" />

During the breeding season, parents are restricted to a short foraging area close to their nest and are therefore vulnerable to small regional changes.<ref>(Cullen, J. M., Chambers, L. E., Coutin, P. C., & Dann, P. (2009). Predicting onset and success of breeding in little penguins Eudyptula minor from ocean temperatures. Marine Ecology Progress Series, 378, 269–278.)</ref> La Niña events increasing the sea surface temperature along the New Zealand coastline cause prey such as schooling fish and krill to either become more regionally scarce or migrate to new habitats.<ref name="Flemming">Flemming, S.A., Lalas, C., and [[Yolanda van Heezik|van Heezik, Y.]] (2013) "[http://newzealandecology.org/nzje/new_issues/NZJEcol37_2_199.pdf Little penguin (''Eudyptula minor'') diet at three breeding colonies in New Zealand]". ''New Zealand Journal of Ecology'' '''37''': 199–205 Accessed 30 January 2014.</ref>

Variation in prey abundance and distribution from year to year causes young birds to be washed up dead from starvation or in weak condition.<ref name=":026">{{Cite web |title=Eudyptula minor - Little Penguin |url=http://www.parks.tas.gov.au/indeX.aspX?base=5091 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110315141411/http://www.parks.tas.gov.au/index.aspx?base=5091 |archive-date=15 March 2011 |access-date=27 July 2014 |website=Parks & Wildlife Service, Tasmania}}</ref> This problem is not constrained to young birds, and has been observed throughout the 20th century.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1925-07-14 |title=MY NATURE DIARY |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article244036310 |access-date=2020-04-22 |work=Herald (Melbourne, Vic. : 1861 - 1954) |page=6}}</ref> The breeding season of 1984–1985 in Australia was particularly bad, with minimal breeding success. Eggs were deserted prior to hatching and many chicks starved to death. Malnourished penguin carcasses were found washed up on beaches and the trend continued the following year. In April 1986, approximately 850 dead penguins were found washed ashore in south-western Victoria. The phenomenon was ascribed to lack of available food.<ref name=":9511"/>

There are two seasonal peaks in the discovery of dead little penguins in Victoria. The first follows [[Moulting|moult]] and the second occurs in mid-winter. Moulting penguins are under stress, and some return to the water in a weak condition afterwards.<ref name=":9511"/> Mid-winter marks the season of lowest prey availability, thus increasing the probability of malnutrition and starvation.

In the late 1980s, it was believed that penguins did not compete with the fishing industry, despite [[anchovy]] being commercially caught.<ref name=":9511"/> That assertion was made prior to the establishment and development of South Australia's commercial [[Sardinops|pilchard]] fishery in the 1990s. In [[South Africa]], the overfishing of species of preferred penguin prey has caused [[African penguin]] populations to decline. Overfishing is a potential (but not proven) threat to the Australian little penguin.<ref name=":9511"/>

In 1990, 24 dead penguins were found in the [[Encounter Bay]] area in South Australia during a week spanning late April to early May. A State government park ranger explained that many of the birds were juvenile and had starved after moulting.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1990-05-02 |title=Penguins: No cause for alarm |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article196361374 |access-date=2020-04-21 |work=Times (Victor Harbor, SA : 1987 - 1999) |page=5}}</ref> In 1995 pilchard mass mortality events occurred, which reduced the penguins' available prey and resulted in starvation and breeding failure.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dann |first1=P. |last2=Norman |first2=F. I. |last3=Cullen |first3=J. M. |last4=Neira |first4=F. J. |last5=Chiaradia |first5=A. |year=2000 |title=Mortality and breeding failure of little penguins, Eudyptula minor, in Victoria 1995-96 following a widespread mortality of pilchards, Sardinops sagax |journal=Marine and Freshwater Research |volume=51 |issue=4 |pages=355–362 |doi=10.1071/MF99114 |bibcode=2000MFRes..51..355D |url=https://figshare.com/articles/journal_contribution/22838978 }}</ref> Another similar event occurred in 1999. Both mortality events were attributed to an exotic [[pathogen]] which spread across the entire Australian population of the fish, reducing the breeding biomass by 70%. Crested tern and gannet populations also suffered following these events.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Ward |first1=T. M. |url=https://www.pir.sa.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0006/309309/Stock_assessment_of_Australian_Sardine_Sardinops_sagax_off_South_Australia_2017.pdf |title=Stock Assessment of Australian Sardine (Sardinops sagax) off South Australia 2017 |last2=Smart |first2=J. |last3=Ivey |first3=A. |publisher=SARDI |year=2017 |location=South Australia}}</ref> In 1995, 30 dead penguins were found ashore between Waitpinga and Chiton Rocks in the Encounter Bay area. The birds had suffered severe bacterial infections and the mortalities may have been linked to the mass mortality of pilchards that resulted from the spread of an exotic pathogen that year.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1995-06-30 |title=Penguin deaths mystery |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article196626319 |access-date=2020-04-21 |work=Times (Victor Harbor, SA : 1987 - 1999) |page=1}}</ref> However, the penguins were able to successfully adapt their diet to consist of [[slender sprat]] and [[Lagodon|pigfish]].<ref name="Agnew">Agnew, P., Lalas, C., Wright, J., & Dawson, S. (2015). Variation in breeding success and survival of little penguins Eudyptula minor in response to environmental variation. Marine Ecology Progress Series, 541, 219–229.</ref>

=== Reproduction ===
[[File:Little_Penguin_chick.jpg|thumb|Chick in nest burrow]]
[[File:Eudyptula novaehollandiae family exiting burrow.jpg|thumb|Little penguin (''Eudyptula minor'') family exiting burrow at night, [[Bruny Island]]]]
Australian little penguins reach sexual maturity at different ages. The female matures at two years old and the male at three years old.

Between June and August, males return to shore to renovate or dig new burrows and display to attract a mate for the season. Males compete for partners with their displays. Breeding occurs annually, but the timing and duration of the breeding season varies from location to location and from year to year. Breeding occurs during spring and summer when oceans are most productive and food is plentiful.

Australian little penguins remain faithful to their partner during a breeding season and whilst hatching eggs. At other times of the year, they tend to swap burrows. They exhibit [[Philopatry|site fidelity]] to their nesting colonies and nesting sites over successive years. Little penguins can breed as isolated pairs, in colonies, or semi-colonially.<ref name="Flemming3"/>

==== Nesting ====
Penguins' nests vary depending on the available habitat. They are established close to the sea in sandy burrows excavated by the birds' feet or dug previously by other animals. Nests may also be made in caves, rock crevices, under logs, or in or under a variety of man-made structures including nest boxes, pipes, stacks of wood or timber, and buildings. Nests have been occasionally observed to be shared with [[Prion (bird)|prions]], while some burrows are occupied by [[short-tailed shearwater]]s and little penguins in alternating seasons. In the 1980s, little was known on the subject of competition for burrows between bird species.<ref name=":9511"/>

==== Timing ====
The timing of breeding seasons varies across the species' range. In the 1980s, the first egg laid at a penguin colony on Australia's eastern volcanos as early May or as late as October. Eastern Australian populations (including at Phillip Island, Victoria) lay their eggs from July to December.<ref name=":6">{{Cite web |title=About Little Penguins |url=http://penguinfoundation.org.au/about-little-penguins/#Yearly1 |access-date=3 April 2016 |website=penguinfoundation.org.au}}</ref> In South Australia's [[Gulf St Vincent|Gulf St. Vincent]], eggs are laid between April and October<ref name=":7">Wiebkin, A. S. (2011) [http://www.naturalresources.sa.gov.au/files/sharedassets/adelaide_and_mt_lofty_ranges/plants_and_animals/penguin-2011-rep.pdf Conservation management priorities for little penguin populations in Gulf St Vincent. Report to Adelaide and Mount Lofty Ranges Natural Resources Management Board.] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170821044249/http://www.naturalresources.sa.gov.au/files/sharedassets/adelaide_and_mt_lofty_ranges/plants_and_animals/penguin-2011-rep.pdf|date=21 August 2017}} South Australian Research and Development Institute (Aquatic Sciences), Adelaide. SARDI Publication No. F2011/000188-1. SARDI Research Report Series No.588. 97pp</ref> and south of Perth in Western Australia, peak egg-laying occurred in June and continued until mid-October (based on observations from the 1980s).<ref name=":9511"/>

Male and female birds share incubating and chick-rearing duties. They are the only species of penguin capable of producing more than one clutch of eggs per breeding season, but few populations do so. In ideal conditions, a penguin pair is capable of raising two or even three clutches of eggs over an extended season, which can last between eight and twenty-eight weeks.<ref name=":9511"/>

The one or two (on rare occasions, three) white or lightly mottled brown eggs are laid between one and four days apart. Each egg typically weighs around 55 grams at time of laying.<ref name=":9511"/> Incubation takes up to 36 days. Chicks are brooded for 18–38 days and fledge after 7–8 weeks.<ref name="Flemming3" /> On Australia's east coast, chicks are raised from August to March.<ref name=":6" /> In Gulf St. Vincent, chicks are raised from June through November.<ref name=":7" />

Australian little penguins typically return to their colonies to feed their chicks at dusk. The birds tend to come ashore in small groups to provide some defence against predators, which might otherwise pick off individuals. In Australia, the strongest colonies are usually on cat-free and fox-free islands. However, the population on [[Granite Island (South Australia)|Granite Island]] (which is a fox, cat, and dog-free island) has been severely depleted, from around 2000 penguins in 2001 down to 22 in 2015. Granite Island is connected to the mainland via a timber causeway.

==== Double brooding ====
If penguins produce a second clutch of eggs in a season once the first chicks have fledged, this is known as double brooding.<ref name="P Agnew">(Agnew, P., Houston, D., Lalas, C. et al. Variation in reproductive performance of Little Penguins (Eudyptula minor) attributable to double brooding. J Ornithol 155, 101–109 (2014).)</ref> Thus far this behaviour has only been observed in the ''Eudyptula novaehollandiae'', the lineage of little blue penguins which inhabit Australian and Otago regions. There is no evidence to suggest this is an established behaviour within ''Eudyptula minor'', however double broods are occasionally noticed among the colonies in the Kaikōura coastline.<ref>(Rowe, L. K., Weir, J. S., & Judkins, A. G. (2020). Breeding of little penguins (Eudyptula minor), including multiple brooding, at South Bay, Kaikōura, New Zealand, 2006–2017. Notornis, 67(2), 451-458.)</ref>  It is as yet unclear whether this means double brooding is a genetically mediated behaviour. A study carried out on Oamaru penguin colony found double broods to increase breeding success by up to 75% per season.<ref name="P Agnew"/> Double brooding is more likely to occur in individuals who lay their first clutch, prior to mid-September.<ref name="Mattern"/> While there is some interannual variability, the most common period for little penguins to lay their first clutch is in spring, mid-September is considered early and gives individuals time left in the season to lay a second clutch of eggs after the first have fledged.<ref>(Chambers, L. E. (2004). Delayed breeding in Little Penguins–evidence of climate change. Australian Meteorological Magazine, 53(1), 13-19.)</ref>

The onset of double brooding can be strongly influenced by sea surface temperature, age and food availability.<ref>(Grosser, S., Burridge, C. P., Peucker, A. J., & Waters, J. M. (2015). Coalescent Modelling Suggests Recent Secondary-Contact of Cryptic Penguin Species. PloS one, 10(12), e0144966.)</ref> Warmer sea surface temperature in summer and autumn correlated with earlier laying of first clutch of eggs increasing the chances of double brooding.<ref 
name="P Agnew"/>  In contrast, in New Zealand it was observed that during the La Niña phase of the [[El Niño Southern Oscillation]] when colder temperature water was brought to the surface, there was a delay in the onset of breeding for ''Eudyptula novaehollandiae'', thus resulting in a lower incidence of double brooding in the Otago colonies.<ref>(Cullen, J. M., Chambers, L. E., Coutin, P. C., & Dann, P. (2009). Predicting onset and success of breeding in little penguins Eudyptula minor from ocean temperatures. Marine Ecology Progress Series, 378, 269–278.).</ref> Age is also believed to be a factor affecting double brooding because the pairs successfully able to double brood were most commonly strategic in reclaiming successful nests and pair-bonds. Little penguins show a high nest fidelity, and the ability to reclaim success early suggests it is likely that successful double brooding is a behaviour that improves with age. Another influencing factor is the availability of food, for larger colonies such as the Philip Islands, competition for food can increase significantly during the breeding season, particularly if there is variability in the amount of prey available.<ref name="Mattern"/> If this competition results in aggression between adults, this can also influence ability to successfully raise chicks, and successfully breed in the next season.

=== Native predators ===
Predation by native animals is not considered a threat to little penguin populations, as these predators' diets are diverse. In Australia, large native reptiles including the [[tiger snake]] and [[Rosenberg's monitor]]<ref name=":822">{{Cite journal |last1=Colombelli-Négrel |first1=Diane |last2=Tomo |first2=Ikuko |year=2017 |title=Identification of terrestrial predators at two Little Penguin colonies in South Australia |url=http://www.birdlife.org.au/afo/index.php/afo/article/view/2066 |journal=Australian Field Ornithology |language=en-US |volume=34 |pages=1–9 |doi=10.20938/afo34001009 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> are known to take little penguin chicks and [[Tiliqua scincoides|common blue-tongued skinks]] are known to take eggs.<ref name=":9511"/> At sea, Australian little penguins are eaten by [[Arctocephalus forsteri|long-nosed fur seals]]. A study conducted by researchers from the [[South Australian Research and Development Institute]] found that roughly 40 percent of seal droppings in South Australia's [[Granite Island (South Australia)|Granite Island]] area contained little penguin remains.<ref>[http://www.environment.sa.gov.au/data/press/penguins.pdf Penguins]—Environment, South Australian Government {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091020165619/http://www.environment.sa.gov.au/data/press/penguins.pdf|date=20 October 2009}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Littlely |first=Bryan |date=10 October 2007 |title=Fur seals threat to Granite Island penguins |newspaper=The Advertiser |page=23}}</ref> Other marine predators include [[Australian sea lion]]s, [[shark]]s, and [[Thyrsites|barracouta]].<ref name=":9511"/>

The introduction of [[Tasmanian devil]]s to [[Maria Island]] in 2012 led to the complete destruction of a population of Australian little penguins that numbered 3,000 breeding pairs before the introduction.<ref>{{Cite news |date=21 June 2021 |title=Tasmanian devils devastate penguin population on Australian island |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-australia-57558396 |work=BBC News}}</ref>

Australian little penguins are also preyed upon by [[white-bellied sea eagle]]s. These large birds-of-prey are endangered in South Australia and not considered a threat to colony viability there. Other avian predators include: [[kelp gull]]s, [[pacific gull]]s, [[brown skua]]s, and [[currawong]]s.<ref name=":9511"/>

In Victoria, at least one penguin death has been attributed to a [[Rakali|water rat]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Preston |first=T. |date=2008-12-01 |title=Water rats as predators of little penguins |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/287053872 |journal=Victorian Naturalist |volume=125 |pages=165–168}}</ref>

==== Parasites ====
Australian little penguins play an important role in the ecosystem as not only a predator to parasites but also a host. Recent studies have shown a new species of feather mite that feeds on the preening oil on the feathers of the penguin.<ref name="E.M.1">{{cite web |author=Ashley Chung |title=Eudyptula minor Little Penguin |url=http://animaldiversity.ummz.umich.edu/accounts/Eudyptula_minor/ |access-date=9 July 2013 |publisher=Animal Diversity Web}}</ref> Australian little penguins preen their mates to strengthen social bonds and remove parasites, especially from their partner's head where self-preening is difficult.<ref name=":9511"/>

== Relationship with humans ==
[[File:Fairy_penguin_feeding_-_melbourne_zoo.jpg|thumb|Feeding time at [[Melbourne Zoo]]]]
Australian little penguins have long been a curiosity to humans.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1995-11-17 |title=Elliot kids love penguins |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article196385622 |access-date=2020-04-21 |work=Times (Victor Harbor, SA : 1987 - 1999) |page=14}}</ref> Captive animals are often exhibited in zoos. Over time attitudes towards penguins have evolved from direct exploitation (for meat, skins, and eggs) to the development of tourism ventures, conservation management and the protection of both birds and their habitat.

=== Direct exploitation ===
During the 19th and 20th centuries, Australian little penguins were shot for sport, killed for their skins, captured for amusement and eaten by ship-wrecked sailors and castaways to avoid starvation.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Hay |first=Alexander |date=24 September 1949 |title=Days of Misery on Barren Isle |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article56058797 |access-date=28 July 2014 |newspaper=The Mail |location=Adelaide, South Australia}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=19 September 1949 |title=S.A. Pair Marooned on Barren Island |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article36687605 |access-date=28 July 2014 |newspaper=The Advertiser |location=Adelaide, South Australia}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=28 November 1896 |title=Esperance news by telegraph. Loss of the Fleetwing |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article163903445 |access-date=7 December 2014 |newspaper=The Norseman Pioneer}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=1899-05-13 |title=STORY OF THE PENGUINS. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article29501157 |access-date=2020-04-21 |work=Advertiser (Adelaide, SA : 1889 - 1931) |page=6}}</ref> Their eggs were also collected for human consumption by indigenous<ref name=":9511"/> and non-indigenous people.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1903-04-19 |title=PORT POINTS. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article57222015 |access-date=2020-04-21 |work=Sunday Times (Perth, WA : 1902 - 1954) |page=2}}</ref> In 1831, N. W. J. Robinson noted that penguins were typically soaked in water for many days to tenderise the meat before eating.<ref name=":9511"/>

One of the colonies raided for penguin skins was [[Lady Julia Percy Island]] in Victoria.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1941-08-08 |title=DESTRUCTION OF SEALS |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article8197106 |access-date=2020-04-22 |work=Argus (Melbourne, Vic. : 1848 - 1957) |page=2}}</ref> The following directions for preparing penguin skin were published in ''The Chronicle'' in 1904:<ref>{{Cite news |date=1904-09-10 |title=THE PORT LINCOLN RAILWAY. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article88377685 |access-date=2020-04-21 |work=Chronicle (Adelaide, SA : 1895 - 1954) |page=30}}</ref><blockquote>'F.W.M.,' [[Port Lincoln]]. — To clean penguin skins, scrape off as much fat as you can with a blunt knife. Then peg the skin out carefully, stretching it well. Let it remain in the sun till most of the fat is dried out of it, then rub with a compound of powdered alum, salt, and pepper in about equal proportions. Continue to rub this on at intervals until the skin becomes soft and pliable.</blockquote>An Australian taxidermist was once commissioned to make a woman's hat for a cocktail party from the remains of a dead little penguin. The newspaper described it as "a smart little [[toque]] of white and black feathers, with black flippers set at a jaunty angle on the crown."<ref>{{Cite news |date=1939-06-10 |title=Taxidermist Cuts His Cloth To Suit The Times |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article205633852 |access-date=2020-04-22 |work=Age (Melbourne, Vic. : 1854 - 1954) |page=12}}</ref>

In the 20th century, little penguins were maliciously attacked by humans, used as bait to catch [[Jasus edwardsii|southern rock lobster]],<ref name=":9511"/> used to free snagged fishing tackle,<ref>{{Cite news |date=1939-02-24 |title=Port Lincoln's Islands—Sir Joseph Bank's Group |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article96741372 |access-date=2020-06-02 |work=Port Lincoln Times (SA : 1927 - 1954) |page=7}}</ref> killed as incidental bycatch by fishermen using nets, and killed by vehicle strikes on roads and on the water.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Pim |first=Mr. |date=2 March 1951 |title=Passing By |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article130372307 |access-date=28 July 2014 |newspaper=News |location=Adelaide, South Australia}}</ref> However, towards the end of the 20th century and the beginning of the 21st, more mutually beneficial relationships between penguins and humans developed. The sites of some breeding colonies have become carefully managed tourist destinations which provide an economic boost for coastal and island communities in Australia and New Zealand. These locations also often provide facilities and volunteer staff to support population surveys, habitat improvement works and little penguin research programs.

=== Tourism ===
At [[Phillip Island (Victoria)|Phillip Island]], Victoria, a viewing area has been established at the [[Phillip Island Nature Park]] to allow visitors to view the nightly "penguin parade". Lights and concrete stands have been erected to allow visitors to see but not photograph or film the birds (this is because it can blind or scare them) interacting in their colony.<ref>{{cite web |author=Tourism Victoria |title=Phillip Island Penguin Parade |url=http://www.visitvictoria.com/Regions/Phillip-Island/Activities-and-attractions/Nature-and-wildlife/Wildlife-viewing/Penguins.aspx |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130123224958/http://www.visitvictoria.com/Regions/Phillip-Island/Activities-and-attractions/Nature-and-wildlife/Wildlife-viewing/Penguins.aspx |archive-date=23 January 2013 |access-date=21 July 2011 |work=Visit Victoria}}</ref> In 1987, more international visitors viewed the penguins coming ashore at Phillip Island than visited [[Uluru]]. In the financial year 1985–86, 350,000 people saw the event, and at that time audience numbers were growing 12% annually.<ref name=":922">{{Cite book |last1=Stahel |first1=Colin |title=Little Penguin - Fairy Penguins in Australia |last2=Gales |first2=Rosemary |publisher=New South Wales University Press |year=1987 |page=108}}</ref>

In [[Bicheno, Tasmania]], evening penguin viewing tours are offered by a local tour operator at a rookery on private land.<ref>[http://www.discovertasmania.com/tour/bichenopenguintours Tourism Tasmania > Bicheno Penguin Tours] Accessed 16 September 2013.</ref> A similar sunset tour is offered at [[Low Head]], near the mouth of the [[Kanamaluka / Tamar River|Tamar River]] on Tasmania's north coast.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Welcome |url=https://www.penguintourstasmania.com.au/ |access-date=2018-08-23 |website=Low Head Penguin Tours |language=en-AU}}</ref> Observation platforms exist near some of Tasmania's other little penguin colonies, including Stanley, [[Bruny Island]] and Lillico Beach near [[Devonport, Tasmania|Devonport]].<ref>{{Cite news |date=2017-01-20 |title=Where to see little penguins in Tasmania |url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2017-01-20/how-to-see-little-penguins-in-tasmania/8197000 |access-date=2018-08-23 |work=ABC News |language=en-AU}}</ref>

South of [[Perth, Western Australia]], visitors to [[Penguin Island (Western Australia)|Penguin Island]] are able to view penguin feeding within a penguin rehabilitation centre and may also encounter wild penguins ashore in their natural habitat. The island is accessible via a short passenger ferry ride, and visitors depart the island before dusk to protect the colony from disturbance.

Visitors to [[Kangaroo Island]], South Australia, have nightly opportunities to observe penguins at the Kangaroo Island Marine Centre in Kingscote and at the Penneshaw Penguin Centre.<ref>{{cite web |title=Penneshaw Penguin Centre |url=http://www.tourkangarooisland.com.au/search/results.aspx?dw_op=gp&dw_pid=9005545&dw_tid= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120415085731/http://www.tourkangarooisland.com.au/search/results.aspx?dw_op=gp&dw_pid=9005545&dw_tid= |archive-date=15 April 2012 |access-date=12 June 2012 |publisher=Tourkangarooisland.com.au}}</ref> [[Granite Island (South Australia)|Granite Island]] at [[Victor Harbor, South Australia]] continues to offer guided tours at dusk, despite its colony dropping from thousands in the 1990s to dozens in 2014.<ref>{{cite web |title=Granite Island Recreation & Nature Park: Penguin Tours South Australia |url=http://www.graniteisland.com.au/html/penguin_tours.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120527154135/http://www.graniteisland.com.au/html/penguin_tours.html |archive-date=27 May 2012 |access-date=12 June 2012 |publisher=Graniteisland.com.au}}</ref> There is also a Penguin Centre located on the island where the penguins can be viewed in captivity.<ref>{{cite web |title=Granite Island Penguin Centre: Looking after the Little Penguins of South Australia |url=http://www.graniteisland.com.au/html/penguin_centre.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120320174644/http://www.graniteisland.com.au/html/penguin_centre.html |archive-date=20 March 2012 |access-date=12 June 2012 |publisher=Graniteisland.com.au}}</ref>

In the [[Otago]], New Zealand town of [[Oamaru]], visitors view the birds returning to their colony at dusk.<ref>{{cite web |title=Oamaru Blue Penguin Colony |url=http://www.penguins.co.nz |access-date=12 June 2012 |publisher=Penguins.co.nz}}</ref> In Oamaru it is common for penguins to nest within the cellars and foundations of local shorefront properties, especially in the old historic precinct of the town. Little penguin viewing facilities have been established at [[Taiaroa Head|Pilots Beach]] on the [[Otago Peninsula]] in [[Dunedin]]. Here visitors are guided by volunteer wardens to watch penguins returning to their burrows at dusk.<ref>{{cite web |title=Blue Penguins Pukekura |url=http://www.bluepenguins.co.nz |access-date=12 June 2012 |publisher=Bluepenguins.co.nz}}</ref>

=== Mascots and logos ===
[[Linus Torvalds]], the original creator of [[Linux]] (a popular operating system [[Kernel (operating system)|kernel]]), was once pecked by an Australian little penguin while on holiday in Australia. Reportedly, this encounter encouraged Torvalds to select [[Tux (mascot)|Tux]] as the official Linux mascot.<ref>{{cite web |title="Tux" the Aussie Penguin |url=http://www.linux.org.au/linux/tux |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060507115127/http://www.linux.org.au/linux/tux |archive-date=7 May 2006 |access-date=25 June 2006 |publisher=Linux Australia}}</ref>

A [[Linux]] kernel programming challenge called the Eudyptula Challenge has attracted thousands of persons; its creator(s) use the name "Little Penguin".<ref>{{cite web |title=The Eudyptula Challenge |url=http://eudyptula-challenge.org/}}</ref>

Penny the Little Penguin was the mascot for the 2007 [[FINA]] World Swimming Championships held in Melbourne, Victoria.<ref>{{cite web |title=FINA |url=http://melbourne2007.com.au/site/sections/inquiry/inquiry_penny_home.html |access-date=12 June 2012 |publisher=Melbourne, 2007}}</ref><ref>[http://www.dse.vic.gov.au/DSE/nrenpa.nsf/LinkView/C64420AFE15676A9CA2571FA007DC400BC992A0BE8BCE1E24A256DEA002432FD Protecting our Little Penguins (Victorian Government website)] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080721104802/http://www.dse.vic.gov.au/DSE/nrenpa.nsf/LinkView/C64420AFE15676A9CA2571FA007DC400BC992A0BE8BCE1E24A256DEA002432FD|date=21 July 2008}}</ref>

== Threats ==

=== Introduced predators ===
Introduced mammalian predators present the greatest terrestrial risk to little penguins and include cats, dogs, rats, foxes, ferrets, and [[stoats]].<ref name="pengsent2">{{cite web |last=Grabski |first=Valerie |year=2009 |title=Little Penguin - Penguin Project |url=http://mesh.biology.washington.edu/penguinProject/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111216162812/http://mesh.biology.washington.edu/penguinProject/Little |archive-date=16 December 2011 |access-date=25 November 2011 |publisher=Penguin Sentinels/University of Washington}}</ref><ref name="penguins-cl2">{{cite web |last=Dann |first=Peter |title=Penguins: Little (Blue or Fairy) Penguins - Eudyptula minor |url=http://www.penguins.cl/little-penguins.htm |access-date=25 November 2011 |publisher=International Penguin Conservation Work Group}}</ref><ref name="bbc">{{cite web |date=July 2005 |title=BBC - Science & Nature -Sea Life - Fact Files: Little/Fairy penguin |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/nature/blueplanet/factfiles/birds/fairy_penguin_bg.shtml |archive-url=https://archive.today/20130113231952/http://www.bbc.co.uk/nature/blueplanet/factfiles/birds/fairy_penguin_bg.shtml |archive-date=13 January 2013 |access-date=25 November 2011 |publisher=BBC}}</ref><ref name=":52">{{Cite news |date=26 June 2015 |title=Fox thought to have killed nearly 30 penguins shot overnight |url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2015-06-27/fox-thought-to-have-killed-nearly-30-penguins-shot-overnight/6577510 |access-date=27 June 2015 |website=ABC News}}</ref><ref>[http://www.abc.net.au/news/2017-02-03/cape-northumberland-penguin-colony-in-decline/8238716 Penguin-spotting encouraged after predator attack wipes out Cape Northumberland colony] ''ABC News'', 3 February 2017. Retrieved 15 November 2019.</ref> Significant dog attacks have been recorded at the colony at Little Kaiteriteri Beach,<ref name=":4">{{Cite news |last=Carson |first=Jonathan |date=3 September 2014 |title=DOC devastated by death of penguins |newspaper=[[The Nelson Mail]] |url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/nelson-mail/news/10455400/DOC-devastated-by-death-of-penguins |access-date=4 September 2014}}</ref> and a suspected [[stoat]] or [[ferret]] attack at Doctor's Point near [[Dunedin]], New Zealand, claimed the lives of 29 little blue penguins in November 2014.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Mead |first=Thomas |date=5 November 2014 |title=Stoat suspected in Little blue penguin massacre |publisher=3 News |url=http://www.3news.co.nz/nznews/stoat-suspected-in-little-blue-penguin-massacre-2014110517 |access-date=11 November 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141111032429/http://www.3news.co.nz/nznews/stoat-suspected-in-little-blue-penguin-massacre-2014110517 |archive-date=11 November 2014}}</ref>

==== Dogs and cats ====
Uncontrolled dogs or [[Cats in Australia|feral cats]] can have sudden and severe impacts on penguin colonies (more than the penguin's natural predators) and may kill many individuals. Examples of colonies affected by dog attacks include [[Manly, New South Wales|Manly]], New South Wales,<ref name=":12">{{Cite news |last=Holland |first=Malcolm |date=6 December 2010 |title=Seven penguins found dead at Manly |url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/seven-penguins-found-dead-at-manly/story-e6freuy9-1225966485353 |access-date=4 September 2014 |newspaper=The Daily Telegraph}}</ref> [[Penneshaw, South Australia|Penneshaw]], South Australia,<ref name=":2">{{Cite news |date=10 July 1984 |title=Dogs kill penguins |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article127213168 |access-date=4 September 2014 |newspaper=The Canberra Times}}</ref> Red Chapel Beach,<ref name=":3">{{Cite news |date=12 March 2003 |title=Penguins killed in dog attack |url=http://www.smh.com.au/articles/2003/03/12/1047431087356.html |access-date=4 September 2014 |newspaper=The Sydney Morning Herald}}</ref> [[Wynyard, Tasmania|Wynyard]],<ref name=":1122">{{Cite news |last1=Carter |first1=Mahalia |last2=Dadson |first2=Manika |date=2019-09-15 |title=Tasmanian Government boosts fines for wildlife slaughter |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2019-09-15/penguin-dog-death-penalties-increased/11514358 |access-date=2020-04-22 |website=ABC News |language=en-AU}}</ref> [[Camdale, Tasmania|Camdale]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=Powell |first=Meg |date=2020-07-20 |title=Fifth little penguin carcass found at Camdale |url=https://www.theadvocate.com.au/story/6841308/fifth-little-penguin-carcass-found-at-camdale/ |access-date=2020-07-20 |website=The Advocate |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Powell |first=Meg |date=2020-07-22 |title=DPIPWE releases results from little penguin investigation |url=https://www.theadvocate.com.au/story/6845603/dpipwe-releases-results-from-little-penguin-investigation/ |access-date=2020-07-23 |website=The Advocate |language=en}}</ref> and [[Low Head]] in Tasmania,<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 October 2018 |title=Almost 60 penguins killed in suspected dog attack in Tasmania, months after similar incident |url=https://mobile.abc.net.au/news/2018-10-17/penguins-killed-at-low-head-tasmania-in-repeat-of-earlier-attack/10386460?pfmredir=sm |access-date=2018-10-18 |publisher=Australian Broadcasting Corporation |language=en}}</ref> [[Penguin Island (Western Australia)|Penguin Island]] in Western Australia, and Little Kaiteriteri Beach in New Zealand.<ref name=":42">{{Cite news |last=Carson |first=Jonathan |date=3 September 2014 |title=DOC devastated by death of penguins |url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/nelson-mail/news/10455400/DOC-devastated-by-death-of-penguins |access-date=4 September 2014 |newspaper=The Nelson Mail}}</ref> Paw prints at an attack site at Freeman's Knob, Encounter Bay, South Australia showed that the dog responsible was small, roughly the size of a terrier. The single attack may have rendered the small colony extinct.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1981-08-12 |title=Penguin colony attacked |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article187382181 |access-date=2020-04-21 |work=Victor Harbour Times (SA : 1932 - 1986) |page=1}}</ref> Cats have been recorded preying on penguin chicks at Emu Bay on Kangaroo Island in South Australia.<ref name=":822"/> In October 2011, 15 dead penguin chicks were found near the Kingscote colony with their heads removed. A dog or cat attack was presumed to be the cause of death. A similar event also occurred in 2010.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Parks |first1=Phillip Island Nature |date=2011-10-06 |title=Penguin chicks killed on Kangaroo Island |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2011-10-07/penguins-census-count-kangaroo-island/3337498 |access-date=2020-06-29 |newspaper=ABC News |publisher=Australian Broadcasting Corporation |language=en-AU}}</ref>

The threat of dog and cat attack is ongoing at many colonies and reports of dog attacks on penguins date back to the mid 20th century.<ref name=":1222">{{Cite news |date=1951-02-20 |title=A bit big for a flat |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article229184872 |access-date=2020-07-17 |work=Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 - 1954) |page=5}}</ref> In the first seven months of 2014, South Australian animal rescue organisation AMWRRO received and treated 22 penguins that had been injured during dog attacks.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Another Little Penguin fall's victim to dogs running free {{!}} Australian Marine Wildlife Research & Rescue Organisation – AMWRRO - |url=http://www.amwrro.org.au/in-care/another-little-penguin-falls-victim-to-dogs-running-free |access-date=2020-07-12 |language=en-US}}</ref>

==== Foxes ====
Foxes have been known to prey on little penguins since at least the early 20th century.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1924-03-22 |title=FOXES OF PHILLIP ISLAND. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article140750657 |access-date=2020-04-22 |work=Australasian (Melbourne, Vic. : 1864 - 1946) |page=59}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=1953-08-25 |title=Penguins are plentiful |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article249251177 |access-date=2020-04-22 |work=Herald (Melbourne, Vic. : 1861 - 1954) |page=7}}</ref> A fox was believed responsible for the deaths of 53 little penguins over several nights on Granite Island in 1994.<ref>{{Cite news |date=11 March 1994 |title=Penguin slaughter |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article196619103 |access-date=17 August 2015 |newspaper=Times |page=1}}</ref> Little penguins on Middle Island off [[Warrnambool, Victoria]] have suffered heavy predation by [[fox]]es, which were able to reach the island at low [[tide]] by a tidal sand bridge. The small colony was reduced from approximately 600 penguins in 2001 to less than 10 in 2005.<ref name="softpedia22">{{cite web |last=Vieru |first=Tudor |date=7 January 2009 |title=Sheepdogs Guard Endangered Fairy Penguin Colony |url=http://news.softpedia.com/news/Sheepdogs-to-Guard-Endangered-Fairy-Penguin-Colony-101357.shtml |access-date=25 November 2011 |publisher=Softpedia}}</ref> The use of Maremma sheepdogs to guard the colony has helped it recover to 100 birds by 2017.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wallis |first1=Robert |last2=King |first2=Kristie |last3=Wallis |first3=Anne |year=2017 |title=The Little Penguin'Eudyptula minor'on Middle Island, Warrnambool, Victoria: An update on population size and predator management. |journal=Victorian Naturalist |volume=134 |issue=2 |pages=48–51}}</ref> In June 2015, 26 penguins from the Manly colony were killed in 11 days. A fox believed responsible was eventually shot in the area and an autopsy was expected to prove or disprove its involvement.<ref name=":52"/> In November 2015 a fox entered the little penguin enclosure at the Melbourne Zoo and killed 14 penguins, prompting measures to further "fox proof" the enclosure.<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 November 2015 |title=Keeps on lookout for fox which killed 14 penguins at Melbourne Zoo |url=http://www.9news.com.au/national/2015/11/04/12/15/14-penguins-found-dead-in-melbourne-zoo |access-date=4 November 2015 |website=news}}</ref>

==== Other animals ====
A suspected [[stoat]] or [[ferret]] attack at Doctor's Point near [[Dunedin]], New Zealand claimed the lives of 29 little blue penguins in November 2014.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Mead |first=Thomas |date=5 November 2014 |title=Stoat suspected in Little blue penguin massacre |url=http://www.3news.co.nz/nznews/stoat-suspected-in-little-blue-penguin-massacre-2014110517 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141111032429/http://www.3news.co.nz/nznews/stoat-suspected-in-little-blue-penguin-massacre-2014110517 |archive-date=11 November 2014 |access-date=11 November 2014 |publisher=3 News}}</ref> A population of [[Tasmanian devil]]s introduced to [[Maria Island]] in 2012 for conservation reasons led to the loss of the local little penguin colony.<ref name="devil">{{cite web |last= |first= |date=26 June 2021 |title=Introduced Tasmanian Devils wipe out Little Penguin population |url=https://www.birdguides.com/news/introduced-tasmanian-devils-wipe-out-little-penguin-population/ |access-date=26 June 2021 |publisher=BirdGuides}}</ref>

=== Oil spills ===
[[Oil spill]]s can be lethal for penguins and other sea birds and events related ports and shipping have [[List of oil spills impacting penguins|impacted penguins]] across the [[Southern Hemisphere]] since the 1920s.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Little Penguins |url=https://penguin-pedia.com/penguin-species/little-penguins/ |access-date=2019-07-08 |website=Penguin Pedia |language=en-US}}</ref> Oil is toxic when ingested and penguins' buoyancy and the insulative quality of their plumage is damaged by contact with oil.<ref name=":026"/><ref name=":9511"/> Little penguin populations have been significantly affected during two major oil spills at sea: the [[MV Iron Baron (1985)|''Iron Baron'' oil spill]] off Tasmania's north coast in 1995 and the [[Rena oil spill|grounding of the ''Rena'']] off New Zealand in 2011. In 2005, a 10-year post-mortem reflection on the ''Iron Baron'' incident estimated penguin fatalities at 25,000.<ref>[http://www.abc.net.au/news/2005-07-10/shipping-industry-reflects-on-iron-baron-oil-spill/2054904 "Shipping industry reflects on Iron Baron spill"] ''ABC News'' (10 July 2005). Retrieved 2014-01-27.</ref> The ''Rena'' incident killed 2,000 seabirds (including little penguins) directly, and killed an estimated 20,000 in total based on wider ecosystem impacts.<ref name="Backhouse2">{{cite news |last=Backhouse |first=Matthew |date=28 December 2011 |title=Penguin reigns in battle for nation's hearts |url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/news/article.cfm?c_id=1&objectid=10775569 |access-date=28 March 2012 |work=[[The New Zealand Herald]]}}</ref><ref name="The New Zealand Herald">{{cite news |date=25 November 2011 |title=Rena: Oil clean-up chemical worries Greenpeace |url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/rena-oil-spill/news/article.cfm?c_id=1503203&objectid=10768517 |access-date=28 March 2012 |work=[[The New Zealand Herald]]}}</ref>

Victoria's coastline has been subjected to chronic oil contamination from minor discharges or spills which have impacted little penguins at several colonies. An oil spill or dumping event claimed the lives of up to 120 little penguins which were found oiled, deceased and ashore near [[Warrnambool]] in 1990. A further 104 penguins were taken into care for cleaning. The waters west of Cape Otway were polluted with bunker oil. The source was unknown at the time and an investigation was started into three potentially responsible vessels.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1990-05-27 |title=Penguins still dying from slick oil |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article122249790 |access-date=2020-04-22 |work=Canberra Times (ACT : 1926 - 1995) |page=3}}</ref>

Earlier oil spill or oil dumping events have injured or killed little penguins at various locations in the 1920s,<ref>{{Cite news |date=1925-08-14 |title=A Naturalist's Jottings |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article73504833 |access-date=2020-04-22 |work=Frankston and Somerville Standard (Vic. : 1921 - 1939) |page=7}}</ref> 1930s,<ref>{{Cite news |date=1934-08-03 |title=Discharge of Oil From Ships Kills Sea Birds |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article74104524 |access-date=2020-04-21 |work=Advertiser (Adelaide, SA : 1931 - 1954) |page=13}}</ref> 1940s,<ref>{{Cite news |date=1945-09-09 |title=Oil kills penguin |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article229013388 |access-date=2020-07-17 |work=Sun (Sydney, NSW : 1910 - 1954) |page=6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=1940-11-07 |title=Oil Kills Penquins. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article74941444 |access-date=2020-04-21 |work=Northern Times (Carnarvon, WA : 1905 - 1952) |page=5}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=1946-07-16 |title=OIL ON BAY KILLS MANY PENGUINS |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article245550059 |access-date=2020-04-22 |work=Herald (Melbourne, Vic. : 1861 - 1954) |page=5}}</ref> 1950s,<ref>{{Cite news |date=1951-06-22 |title=OILED-UP PENGUIN COMES ASHORE |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article131347106 |access-date=2020-07-17 |work=Dubbo Liberal and Macquarie Advocate (NSW : 1894 - 1954) |page=1}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=1954-07-07 |title=Oil causing big toll of penguins on bayside |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article248332456 |access-date=2020-04-22 |work=Herald (Melbourne, Vic. : 1861 - 1954) |page=9}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=1954-07-09 |title=Oily penguin. Soothing (Australia, 1954) |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/54839815/oily-penguin-soothing-australia-1954/ |access-date=2020-07-06 |work=The Age |page=2}}</ref> 1960s<ref>{{Cite news |date=1969-03-11 |title=Stray oiled penguin thinks it's all a little fishy (Australia, 1969) |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/54839583/stray-oiled-penguin-thinks-its-all-a/ |access-date=2020-07-06 |work=The Sydney Morning Herald |page=1}}</ref> and 1970s.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1992-12-17 |title=Oil tanker spill will wipe out penguins (1992 account of 1971 event) |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/54584607/oil-tanker-spill-will-wipe-out-penguins/ |access-date=2020-07-07 |work=The Age |page=11}}</ref> The threat persists in the 21st century, with oiled birds received for treatment at specialised facilities like AMWRRO in South Australia.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Bateman |first=Ivy |date=2009-03-24 |title=Oil covered penguins still not back on their happy feet |url=https://www.abc.net.au/local/stories/2009/03/24/2524784.htm |access-date=2020-07-12 |publisher=Australian Broadcasting Corporation |language=en-AU}}</ref> Oil spills are the most common cause of the little penguins being admitted to the rehabilitation facilities at Phillip Island Nature Park (PINP).
